title: Chilli con carne
subtitle: Tex-Mex beef and kidney bean stew with dried chillies and cumin
excerpt: A slow-cooked Tex-Mex stew of minced beef, kidney beans, tinned tomatoes, and dried chilli spices. Better the day after it is made, when the flavours have had time to settle. Serve with rice, soured cream, grated Cheddar, and a handful of coriander.
sourceNotes: Chilli con carne developed in Texas in the nineteenth century, likely in San Antonio, and is documented in period American cookery literature and travel accounts from the 1820s onward. It was served at the San Antonio Chili Stand at the 1893 World's Fair in Chicago. Multiple cross-referenced historical Texan food sources.

recipe.freezeNotes: Freeze in portions for up to 3 months. Defrost overnight and reheat gently, adding a splash of water.
recipe.batchNotes: Doubles or triples well. The flavour improves with batch cooking.
recipe.makeAheadNotes: Make 1 to 2 days ahead — the flavour improves significantly overnight.

content.text: The heat level in chilli depends entirely on the chilli powder you use — some are mild, some are sharp. Taste the powder before cooking and adjust the quantity. The smoked paprika adds depth and colour without additional heat. A small square of dark chocolate or a teaspoon of dark brown sugar stirred in at the end rounds the flavour and cuts the acidity of the tinned tomatoes; this is optional but worth trying.

Brown the beef in batches over high heat rather than stirring it continuously. Stirred mince steams; left alone, it browns and develops a crust that adds flavour to the sauce. Allow the mince to colour properly before adding the liquid.

Heat the {{olive-oil}} of olive oil in a large heavy pot over high heat. Add the {{beef-mince}} of beef mince in batches and cook without stirring for 2 to 3 minutes until browned on the bottom. Break it up and stir, then cook for a further 2 minutes until no pink remains. Transfer to a bowl and set aside.

Reduce the heat to medium. In the same pot, cook the {{onion}} onions and {{pepper-red}} red pepper for 8 minutes until softened. Add the {{garlic}} of garlic, {{chilli-powder}} of chilli powder, {{cumin-ground}} of cumin, {{paprika-smoked}} of smoked paprika, and {{oregano-dried}} of oregano. Cook, stirring, for 2 minutes. Add the {{tomato-puree}} of tomato purée and cook for 1 minute.

Return the beef to the pot. Add the {{tinned-tomatoes}} of tinned tomatoes and {{stock-beef}} of beef stock. Season with salt. Bring to the boil, then reduce to a low simmer and cook for 35 to 40 minutes, stirring occasionally, until the sauce is thick and dark. Add the {{kidney-beans}} of kidney beans and cook for a further 10 minutes. If using, add the {{dark-chocolate}} of dark chocolate and stir until melted. Taste and adjust the seasoning. Serve with rice, soured cream, grated Cheddar, and coriander.

items.fix: Simmer with the lid off for the last 15 minutes; the sauce should reduce to a thick, spoonable consistency

items.symptom: Mince is grey and bland rather than browned
items.cause: Pan too cool, too much mince in the pan, or mince stirred continuously
items.fix: Brown in batches over high heat; leave undisturbed for 2 to 3 minutes before breaking up

items.symptom: Chilli tastes flat despite plenty of spice
items.fix: Cook the spices in the dry pan with the aromatics for 1 to 2 minutes before adding liquid; this blooms the spices and develops the flavour

content.text: Chilli con carne means chilli with meat and its origins are in San Antonio, Texas, in the nineteenth century, where it was sold in the streets by women known as the Chili Queens. It spread via the 1893 World's Fair and became one of the defining dishes of Tex-Mex cooking. Texan purists often argue that beans do not belong in chilli, which is an ongoing debate among people who take this seriously. The bean version is what the rest of the world cooks, and it is a good weeknight dish.
